[[画像:Oni no Manaita.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の俎]] [[画像:Oni no Secchin.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の雪隠]]
'''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）と'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）[[明日香村]]平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]で作られた[[遺構]]。
畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は１つの[[古墳]]の石室だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものである。 元々は繰り抜かれた石室（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。
* 底石（俎）は長さ約4.5ｍ、幅約2.7ｍ、厚さ約約1ｍ。 * 石室（雪隠）の内幅は約1.5ｍ、高さ約1.3ｍ。
言い伝えによると、昔、このあたりに鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。
[[Category:奈良県の建築物・観光名所|おにのせっちん]]
{{stub}}

[[画像:Oni no Manaita.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の俎]] [[画像:Oni no Secchin.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の雪隠]]
'''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）と'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）、[[明日香村]]平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]で作られた[[遺構]]。
==詳細== *畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は１つの[[古墳]]の石室だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものである。元々は繰り抜かれた横口式石槨の石室（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。 *大きさ : 底石（俎）は長さ約4.5ｍ、幅約2.7ｍ、厚さ約1ｍ。 : 石室（雪隠）の内幅は約1.5ｍ、高さ約1.3ｍ。
*言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。

[[画像:Oni no Manaita.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の俎]] [[画像:Oni no Secchin.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の雪隠]]
'''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）と'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）、[[明日香村]]平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]で作られた[[遺構]]。
==詳細== *畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は１つの[[古墳]]の石室だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものである。元々は繰り抜かれた横口式石槨の石室（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。 *大きさ : 底石（俎）は長さ約4.5ｍ、幅約2.7ｍ、厚さ約1ｍ。 : 石室（雪隠）の内幅は約1.5ｍ、高さ約1.3ｍ。
*言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。
* 底石（俎）には多数の穴が開けられており、割り取ろうとした形跡がみられる。これは高取城建設の際に、石垣の石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。
[[Category:奈良県の建築物・観光名所|おにのせつちん]] [[Category:明日香村|おにのせつちん]]

[[画像:Oni no Manaita.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の俎]] [[画像:Oni no Secchin.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の雪隠]]
'''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）と'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）、[[明日香村]]平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]で作られた[[遺構]]。
==詳細== *畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は１つの[[古墳]]の石室だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものである。元々は繰り抜かれた横口式石槨の石室（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。 *大きさ : 底石（俎）は長さ約4.5ｍ、幅約2.7ｍ、厚さ約1ｍ。 : 石室（雪隠）の内幅は約1.5ｍ、高さ約1.3ｍ。
*言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。

[[画像:Oni no Manaita.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の俎]] [[画像:Oni no Secchin.JPG|thumb|200px|鬼の雪隠]]
'''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）と'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）、[[明日香村]]平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]で作られた[[遺構]]。
==詳細== *畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は１つの[[古墳]]の石室だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものである。元々は繰り抜かれた横口式石槨の石室（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。 *大きさ : 底石（俎）は長さ約4.5ｍ、幅約2.7ｍ、厚さ約1ｍ。 : 石室（雪隠）の内幅は約1.5ｍ、高さ約1.3ｍ。
*言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。
* 底石（俎）には多数の穴が開けられており、割り取ろうとした形跡がみられる。これは高取城建設の際に、石垣の石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。 *双墓説 :俎の位置は雪隠から見てやや東側にずれている。実は現在の俎の西にもう一つ別の俎があり、明治の頃に小さく割られ庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、現在の雪隠は西側にあったもう一つの俎と組み合わされた石槨だというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥発掘物語」産経新聞</ref>。割られた石は橿原考古学研究所付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。 <references/>
==関連項目== *[[飛鳥の石造物]]
[[Category:奈良県の建築物・観光名所|おにのせつちん]] [[Category:明日香村|おにのせつちん]]

* 底石（俎）には多数の穴が開けられており、割り取ろうとした形跡がみられる。これは高取城建設の際に、石垣の石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。 *双墓説 :俎の位置は雪隠から見てやや東側にずれている。実は現在の俎の西にもう一つ別の俎があり、明治の頃に小さく割られ庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、現在の雪隠は西側にあったもう一つの俎と組み合わされた石槨だというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥発掘物語」産経新聞</ref>。割られた石は橿原考古学研究所付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。 <references/>

==詳細== *畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は1つの[[古墳]]の石室だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものである。元々は繰り抜かれた横口式石槨の石室（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。 *大きさ : 底石（俎）は長さ約4.5ｍ、幅約2.7ｍ、厚さ約1ｍ。 : 石室（雪隠）の内幅は約1.5ｍ、高さ約1.3ｍ。

* 底石（俎）には多数の穴が開けられており、割り取ろうとした形跡がみられる。これは[[高取城]]建設の際に、石垣の石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。 *双墓説 :俎の位置は雪隠から見てやや東側にずれている。実は現在の俎の西にもう一つ別の俎があり、明治の頃に小さく割られ庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、現在の雪隠は西側にあったもう一つの俎と組み合わされた石槨だというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥発掘物語」産経新聞</ref>。割られた石は[[橿原考古学研究所付属博物館]]の屋外に展示されている。 <references/>
==関連項目== *[[飛鳥の石造物]]
[[Category:奈良県の観光地|おにのせつちん]] [[Category:明日香村|おにのせつちん]]

*言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。

revision.comment: 画像更新と宮内庁治定の旨の追加、カテゴリ見直し（古墳時代・飛鳥時代の重複期）。
revision.text:
{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|上：鬼の俎
下：鬼の雪隠}}}} '''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）と'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）・平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]の石造物。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。 ==概要== 畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は1つの[[古墳]]の石室だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものとされる。元々は繰り抜かれた[[横口式石槨]]の石室（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。底石（俎）には多数の穴が開けられており、割り取ろうとした形跡がみられる。これは[[高取城]]建設の際に、石垣の石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。 大きさは次の通り。 * 底石（俎）：長さ約4.5m、幅約2.7m、厚さ約1m。 * 石室（雪隠）：内幅約1.5m、高さ約1.3m。 言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。 なお、この鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠には双墓説がある。この説では、俎の位置は雪隠から見てやや東側にずれることから、実は現在の俎の西にもう一つ別の俎があり、明治の頃に小さく割られ庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、現在の雪隠は西側にあったもう一つの俎と組み合わされた石槨だというのである河上邦彦:「飛鳥発掘物語」産経新聞。割られた石は[[橿原考古学研究所]]付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。 ==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} ==関連項目== {{Commonscat|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin}} *[[飛鳥の石造物]] {{デフォルトソート:おにのせつちん おにのまないた}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の遺跡]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:陵墓陪冢]]

==概要== 畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は1つの[[古墳]]の石室だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものとされる。元々は繰り抜かれた[[横口式石槨]]の石室（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。底石（俎）には多数の穴が開けられており、割り取ろうとした形跡がみられる。これは[[高取城]]建設の際に、石垣の石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。
大きさは次の通り。 * 底石（俎）：長さ約4.5m、幅約2.7m、厚さ約1m。 * 石室（雪隠）：内幅約1.5m、高さ約1.3m。
言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。
なお、この鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠には双墓説がある。この説では、俎の位置は雪隠から見てやや東側にずれることから、実は現在の俎の西にもう一つ別の俎があり、明治の頃に小さく割られ庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、現在の雪隠は西側にあったもう一つの俎と組み合わされた石槨だというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥発掘物語」産経新聞</ref>。割られた石は[[橿原考古学研究所]]付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== {{Commonscat|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin}} *[[飛鳥の石造物]]

大きさは次の通り。 * 底石（俎）：長さ約4.5m、幅約2.7m、厚さ約1m。 * 石室（雪隠）：内幅約1.5m、高さ約1.3m。

言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。

revision.comment: 「石室」と「石槨」の違いに理解がなかったため訂正。また底石のほぞ穴の時期は不明、よって「後世」とした。
revision.text:
{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|鬼の俎（上）・鬼の雪隠（下）}}}} '''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）・'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）・平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]の石造物。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。
==概要== 畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は1つの[[古墳]]の石槨だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものとされる。元々は花崗岩を繰り抜いた[[横口式石槨]]の蓋石（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。底石（俎）には横口式石槨の特徴である扉石を据えた痕跡が見られる。底石にはほぞ穴が並んでいるが、後世に割り取って石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。
大きさは次の通り。 * 底石（俎）：長さ約4.5m、幅約2.7m、厚さ約1m。 * 蓋石（雪隠）：内幅約1.5m、高さ約1.3m。
言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。
なお、この鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠には双墓（ならびばか）説<ref>日本書紀皇極元年（642）条には「予め双墓を今来に造る。一つをば大陵という。大臣の墓とす。一つをば小陵という。入鹿臣の墓とす」とある。</ref>がある。、実は現在の俎の隣接したところにもう一つやや小さ目の底石があったが、明治の頃に小さく割られ民家の庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、隣にあったもう一つの石槨墓と対をなしていたというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥を掘る」講談社</ref>。割られた石は民家から運ばれて現在は[[橿原考古学研究所]]付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== {{Commonscat|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin}} *[[飛鳥の石造物]]

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|鬼の俎（上）・鬼の雪隠（下）}}}} '''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）・'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）・平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]の石造物。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。
==概要== 畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は1つの[[古墳]]の石槨だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものとされる。元々は花崗岩を繰り抜いた[[横口式石槨]]の蓋石（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。底石（俎）には横口式石槨の特徴である扉石を据えた痕跡が見られる。底石にはほぞ穴が並んでいるが、後世に割り取って石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。
大きさは次の通り。 * 底石（俎）：長さ約4.5m、幅約2.7m、厚さ約1m。 * 蓋石（雪隠）：内幅約1.5m、高さ約1.3m。
言い伝えによると、風の森と呼ばれるこの地方に鬼が棲んでおり、通行人を騙してとらえ食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。
なお、この鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠には双墓（ならびばか）説<ref>日本書紀皇極元年（642）条には「予め双墓を今来に造る。一つをば大陵という。大臣の墓とす。一つをば小陵という。入鹿臣の墓とす」とある。</ref>がある。実は現在の俎の隣接したところにもう一つやや小さ目の底石があったが、明治の頃に小さく割られ民家の庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、隣にあったもう一つの石槨墓と対をなしていたというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥を掘る」講談社</ref>。割られた石は民家から運ばれて現在は[[橿原考古学研究所]]付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。

なお、この鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠には双墓（ならびばか）説<ref>日本書紀皇極元年（642）条には「予め双墓を今来に造る。一つをば大陵という。大臣の墓とす。一つをば小陵という。入鹿臣の墓とす」とある。</ref>がある。実は現在の俎の隣接したところにもう一つやや小さ目の底石があったが、明治の頃に小さく割られ民家の庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、隣にあったもう一つの石槨墓と対をなしていたというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥を掘る」講談社</ref>。割られた石は民家から運ばれて現在は[[橿原考古学研究所]]付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。 <gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|俯瞰図（俎・雪隠接合） File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|展開図（俎・雪隠接合） </gallery>

They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Hous..."
revision.text:
{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Yokoguchi-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Yokoguchi-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * **Base stone (Manaita)**: Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * **Lid stone (Secchin)**: Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.

revision.text:
{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Yokoguchi-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Yokoguchi-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

revision.comment: 言い伝えを修正しました。
revision.text:
{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|鬼の俎（上）・鬼の雪隠（下）}}}} '''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）・'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）・平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]の石造物。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。
==概要== 畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は1つの[[古墳]]の石槨だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものとされる。元々は花崗岩を繰り抜いた[[横口式石槨]]の蓋石（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。底石（俎）には横口式石槨の特徴である扉石を据えた痕跡が見られる。底石にはほぞ穴が並んでいるが、後世に割り取って石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。
大きさは次の通り。 * 底石（俎）：長さ約4.5m、幅約2.7m、厚さ約1m。 * 蓋石（雪隠）：内幅約1.5m、高さ約1.3m。
言い伝えによると、霧ヶ峰と呼ばれたこの周辺には鬼が住んでおり、通行人に霧を降らせ、迷ったところをとらえ、食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。
なお、この鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠には双墓（ならびばか）説<ref>日本書紀皇極元年（642）条には「予め双墓を今来に造る。一つをば大陵という。大臣の墓とす。一つをば小陵という。入鹿臣の墓とす」とある。</ref>がある。実は現在の俎の隣接したところにもう一つやや小さ目の底石があったが、明治の頃に小さく割られ民家の庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、隣にあったもう一つの石槨墓と対をなしていたというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥を掘る」講談社</ref>。割られた石は民家から運ばれて現在は[[橿原考古学研究所]]付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。 <gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|俯瞰図（俎・雪隠接合） File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|展開図（俎・雪隠接合） </gallery>
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== {{Commonscat|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin}} *[[飛鳥の石造物]]

Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.

There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Yokoguchi-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Yokoguchi-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Yokoguchi-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Yokoguchi-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Yokoguchi-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Yokoguchi-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.

Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.

They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村|12=draft}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=draft}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=draft}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所|12=draft}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. revision.comment: Bot: single‑shot reformat + ill→|12=draft + draft categories + iws + DEFAULTSORT + move HTML comments + meta‑cat check
35 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠 up to rev 103421591
revision.text:
{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|鬼の俎（上）・鬼の雪隠（下）}}}} '''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）・'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）・平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]の石造物。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。
==概要== 畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は1つの[[古墳]]の石槨だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものとされる。元々は花崗岩を繰り抜いた[[横口式石槨]]の蓋石（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。底石（俎）には横口式石槨の特徴である扉石を据えた痕跡が見られる。底石にはほぞ穴が並んでいるが、後世に割り取って石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。
大きさは次の通り。 * 底石（俎）：長さ約4.5m、幅約2.7m、厚さ約1m。 * 蓋石（雪隠）：内幅約1.5m、高さ約1.3m。
言い伝えによると、霧ヶ峰と呼ばれたこの周辺には鬼が住んでおり、通行人に霧を降らせ、迷ったところをとらえ、食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。
なお、この鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠には双墓（ならびばか）説<ref>日本書紀皇極元年（642）条には「予め双墓を今来に造る。一つをば大陵という。大臣の墓とす。一つをば小陵という。入鹿臣の墓とす」とある。</ref>がある。実は現在の俎の隣接したところにもう一つやや小さ目の底石があったが、明治の頃に小さく割られ民家の庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、隣にあったもう一つの石槨墓と対をなしていたというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥を掘る」講談社</ref>。割られた石は民家から運ばれて現在は[[橿原考古学研究所]]付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。 <gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|俯瞰図（俎・雪隠接合） File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|展開図（俎・雪隠接合） </gallery>
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== {{Commonscat|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin}} *[[飛鳥の石造物]]
{{デフォルトソート:おにのまないた おにのせつちん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の遺跡]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:陵墓陪冢]] [[Category:石彫]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] to [[Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|鬼の俎（上）・鬼の雪隠（下）}}}} '''鬼の俎'''（おにのまないた）・'''鬼の雪隠'''（おにのせっちん）は、[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]野口（鬼の俎）・平田（鬼の雪隠）にある[[花崗岩]]の石造物。[[宮内庁]]により[[欽明天皇]][[梅山古墳|檜隈坂合陵]]の[[陪塚|陪冢]]に治定されている。
==概要== 畑の中を通る遊歩道の脇の高台には「鬼の俎」が、遊歩道を挟んだ高台の麓に「鬼の雪隠」がある。両者は直線距離にして数十メートル離れているが、元は1つの[[古墳]]の石槨だったものが、盛土が無くなったうえ、二つに分かれてしまったものとされる。元々は花崗岩を繰り抜いた[[横口式石槨]]の蓋石（鬼の雪隠）とその底石（鬼の俎）であった。底石（俎）には横口式石槨の特徴である扉石を据えた痕跡が見られる。底石にはほぞ穴が並んでいるが、後世に割り取って石材として利用しようとしたためとみられる。
大きさは次の通り。 * 底石（俎）：長さ約4.5m、幅約2.7m、厚さ約1m。 * 蓋石（雪隠）：内幅約1.5m、高さ約1.3m。
言い伝えによると、霧ヶ峰と呼ばれたこの周辺には鬼が住んでおり、通行人に霧を降らせ、迷ったところをとらえ、食べたと云われている。「俎」で調理し、「雪隠」で用を足したという。
なお、この鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠には双墓（ならびばか）説<ref>日本書紀皇極元年（642）条には「予め双墓を今来に造る。一つをば大陵という。大臣の墓とす。一つをば小陵という。入鹿臣の墓とす」とある。</ref>がある。実は現在の俎の隣接したところにもう一つやや小さ目の底石があったが、明治の頃に小さく割られ民家の庭石に転用されたという。すなわち鬼の俎・雪隠古墳は双墓であり、隣にあったもう一つの石槨墓と対をなしていたというのである<ref>河上邦彦:「飛鳥を掘る」講談社</ref>。割られた石は民家から運ばれて現在は[[橿原考古学研究所]]付属博物館の屋外に展示されている。 <gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|俯瞰図（俎・雪隠接合） File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|展開図（俎・雪隠接合） </gallery>
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
==関連項目== {{Commonscat|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin}} *[[飛鳥の石造物]]
{{デフォルトソート:おにのまないた おにのせつちん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:飛鳥時代の遺跡]] [[Category:明日香村の歴史]] [[Category:奈良県の観光地]] [[Category:陵墓陪冢]] [[Category:石彫]]

{{Double image stack|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|ja|明日香村|qq=draft}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District|ja|高市郡|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|qq=draft}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kinmei|qq=}}'s {{ill|Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara Archaeological Institute|qq=}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所附属博物館|10=ja|11=奈良県立橿原考古学研究所附属博物館|comment=trying to get the bot to work with these using this|ja|橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara Archaeological Institute|qq=}} Museum.

revision.text:
{{Multiple image|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takaichi District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. They are designated by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] as associated tombs (陪冢) of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]'s {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]], with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|Kashihara Archaeological Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所附属博物館|10=ja|11=奈良県立橿原考古学研究所附属博物館|comment=trying to get the bot to work with these using this|ja|橿原考古学研究所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashihara Archaeological Institute|qq=}} Museum.

revision.text:
{{Multiple image|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takaichi District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. They are designated by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] as associated tombs (陪冢) of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]'s {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]], with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] Museum.

Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]], with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] Museum.
