<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11673378]] Wikidata name: Oni no Manaita and Oni no Setchin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple image|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takaichi District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. They are designated by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] as associated tombs (陪冢) of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]'s {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]], with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] Museum.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11673378]] Wikidata name: Oni no Manaita and Oni no Setchin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple image|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takaichi District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. They are designated by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] as associated tombs (陪冢) of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]'s {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]], with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11673378]] Wikidata name: Oni no Manaita and Oni no Setchin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple image|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in [[:en:Asuka, Nara|Asuka, Nara]], Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), [[:en:Takaichi District, Nara|Takaichi District]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. They are designated by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] as associated tombs (陪冢) of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]'s {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]], with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the [[:en:The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|Kashihara Archaeological Institute]] Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11673378]] Wikidata name: Oni no Manaita and Oni no Setchin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Multiple image|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District, Nara|en|Takaichi District, Nara|WD=Q1117901|lt=Takaichi District}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}'s {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave Tomb-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Cave Tomb-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

{{translated page|ja|鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠|version=103421591|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]
{{Multiple image|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District, Nara|en|Takaichi District, Nara|WD=Q1117901|lt=Takaichi District}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}'s {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Yokoanabo-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoanabo-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Yokoanabo-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute|1=The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin]][[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]
{{Multiple image|right|Oni-no-Manaita, zenkei.jpg|Oni-no-Secchin, zenkei.jpg|250|{{Center|Oni-no-Manaita (Top) and Oni-no-Secchin (Bottom)}}}} '''Oni-no-Manaita''' (鬼の俎) and '''Oni-no-Secchin''' (鬼の雪隠) are granite stone structures located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara|1=Asuka, Nara}}, Noguchi (Oni-no-Manaita) and Hirata (Oni-no-Secchin), {{ill|Takaichi District, Nara|en|Takaichi District, Nara|WD=Q1117901|lt=Takaichi District|1=Takaichi District, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. They are designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} as associated tombs (陪冢) of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}'s {{ill|Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umeyama Kofun|lt=Umemiyama Kofun|ja|梅山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umemiyama Kofun|qq=}} (Hinokuma Sakai Mausoleum).
== Overview ==
Oni-no-Manaita is situated on an elevated area beside a walking path through farmland, while Oni-no-Secchin lies at the base of the hill across the path. Although separated by a few dozen meters in a straight line, the two are believed to have originally been part of a single stone burial chamber (石槨) of a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}}, with the mound having disappeared and the structure splitting into two. Initially, they formed a {{ill|Yokoanabo-style stone chamber|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoanabo-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}}, with the lid stone (Oni-no-Secchin) and the base stone (Oni-no-Manaita) made from granite. The base stone retains traces of where the door stone of the Yokoanabo-style chamber would have been set.
Notably, the base stone contains several mortise holes, believed to have been added later in an attempt to cut and reuse the stone as construction material.
The dimensions are as follows: * '''Base stone (Manaita)''': Approx. 4.5m in length, 2.7m in width, and 1m in thickness. * '''Lid stone (Secchin)''': Approx. 1.5m in internal width and 1.3m in height.
According to local folklore, an oni (demon) inhabited the Wind Forest area (風の森) and would trick and capture travelers, consuming them. It is said that the oni used the "Manaita" for cooking and the "Secchin" as a lavatory.
There is also a theory that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin represent a double tomb (双墓). According to historical records, an additional, slightly smaller base stone once lay adjacent to the current Manaita but was broken into smaller pieces during the Meiji era and repurposed as decorative stones for a private garden. This theory posits that the Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin Kofun formed one tomb, while the neighboring stone chamber constituted a matching counterpart. The fragments from the adjacent stone chamber were later recovered and are now displayed in the outdoor exhibit of the {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute|1=The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture}} Museum.
<gallery> File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-B.jpg|Overhead view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) File:Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin, 3D-N.jpg|Exploded view (Manaita and Secchin rejoined) </gallery>
